name: Q1043671
abstract: Carmen Leggio (September 30, 1927 – May 17, 2009) was an American jazz tenor saxophonist.

interviews.title: Carmen Leggio interviewed by Monk Rowe, Tarrytown, New York, October 31, 1998

concerts.label: Jack Humphrey Septet/ Maynard Ferguson and His Orchestra/ Sarah Vaughan/ Modern Jazz Quartet
Midnight Performance: Jack Humphrey Septet/ Maynard Ferguson and His Orchestra/ Sarah Vaughan/ Modern Jazz Quartet

about_quotes.speaker: Jake Hanna
about_quotes.evidence: Carmen Leggio, a very, very fine player on that... But we did it as we went along.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jake Hanna interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Ernie Watts
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer asks 'Was it Carmen Leggio or Menza?' regarding section mates, but the interviewee Ernie Watts does not confirm Leggio was in the band, instead listing other musicians (Jay Corre, Bobby Porcelli, Don Menza, etc.).
about_quotes.doc_title: Ernie Watts interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 11, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Keter Betts
about_quotes.evidence: So he brought a drummer from Tarrytown, and a piano player from New Rochelle and myself. And we came down there... We were right around the corner from the Howard Theater... so we spent thirteen weeks
about_quotes.doc_title: Keter Betts interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 11, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] That was Carmen Leggio? >> [INTERVIEWEE] Carmen Leggio . . . [INTERVIEWER] . . . was the tenor player? >> [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Cobb — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Lanny Morgan
about_quotes.evidence: Carmen was a good player. I was familiar with what he did. That band was a lot of fun... Not with me. He was on before me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lanny Morgan interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 14, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Temperley
about_quotes.evidence: Well the saxophone section was strictly Italian. It was Sal Nestico and Joe Romano and Carmen Leggio.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Temperley interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997
